questions.question: In programmed I/O, what does the CPU do while waiting for a slow device?
options: Executes other threads from the run queue
Busy-waits by repeatedly polling the device status register
Switches to DMA mode automatically
Suspends itself until the next clock interrupt
questions.explanation: Programmed I/O (polling) has the CPU spin in a loop reading the status register until the device signals READY. No other work gets done during this time.

questions.question: MMIO maps device registers into:
options: A separate I/O address space accessible only with special instructions
The physical address space, accessible via normal load/store instructions
The virtual address space of the kernel only
A reserved region of the TLB
questions.explanation: With MMIO, device registers occupy addresses in the physical address space. The CPU uses ordinary load and store instructions (with volatile pointers) to read/write them.

questions.question: A DMA controller transfers 1 MB from disk to memory at 200 MB/s. During this transfer, the CPU:
options: Must execute one load instruction per byte transferred
Is free to execute other instructions — it programmed the transfer and waits for one completion interrupt
Polls the DMA status register every cycle
Halts until the transfer finishes
questions.explanation: After programming the DMA controller (source, destination, byte count), the CPU is free. The DMA controller handles the transfer independently and raises a single interrupt on completion.

questions.question: Interrupt coalescing is used to:
options: Prevent higher-priority interrupts from preempting lower-priority ones
Combine multiple interrupt events into a single interrupt to reduce context-switch overhead
Eliminate the need for an interrupt vector table
Ensure every device gets exactly one interrupt per millisecond
questions.explanation: Interrupt coalescing batches multiple events (e.g., network packets) into a single interrupt, reducing the overhead of context save/restore at the cost of slightly increased latency.

questions.question: What problem does DMA (Direct Memory Access) solve?
options: It makes memory access faster
It allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement for each byte
It encrypts data in transit
It provides a virtual memory interface for devices
questions.explanation: Without DMA, the CPU must read/write each byte during I/O transfers (programmed I/O), wasting cycles. DMA lets the device controller transfer entire blocks directly to memory. The CPU sets up the transfer, is free to do other work, and gets an interrupt when complete.
